content:
# Exodus Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32r–32v (PDF pp. 62–63); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 42–43 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_002_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_002_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And a man from the house of Levi went and took a daughter of Levi.

**2** And the woman conceived and bore a son. And she saw him, that he was good, and she hid him three months.

**3** And when she could no longer hide him, she took for him an ark of papyrus and coated it with bitumen and with pitch, and she placed the child in it and set it among the reeds at the bank of the River.

**4** And his sister stationed herself at a distance, to know what would be done to him.

**5** And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the River, and her maidens were walking along the side of the River. And she saw the ark in the midst of the reeds, and she sent her handmaid and took it.

**6** And she opened it and saw him -- the child -- and behold, a boy weeping. And she had compassion on him and said, "This is one of the children of the Hebrews."

**7** And his sister said to the daughter of Pharaoh, "Shall I go and call for you a nursing woman from the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for you?"

**8** And the daughter of Pharaoh said to her, "Go." And the young woman went and called the child's mother.

**9** And the daughter of Pharaoh said to her, "Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages." And the woman took the child and nursed him.

**10** And the child grew, and she brought him to the daughter of Pharaoh, and he became her son. And she called his name Moses, and she said, "For I drew him out of the water."

**11** And it came to pass in those days, when Moses had grown up, that he went out to his brothers and looked upon their burdens. And he saw an Egyptian man striking a Hebrew man, one of his brothers.

**12** And he turned this way and that, and he saw that there was no man. And he struck the Egyptian and hid him in the sand.

**13** And he went out on the second day, and behold, two Hebrew men were struggling together. And he said to the one in the wrong, "Why do you strike your neighbor?"

**14** And he said, "Who set you as a man, a prince and a judge, over us? Do you intend to kill me as you killed the Egyptian?" And Moses was afraid and said, "Surely the matter has become known."

**15** And Pharaoh heard of this matter and sought to kill Moses. And Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh and dwelt in the land of Midian, and he sat down by the well.

**16** Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters, and they came and drew water and filled the troughs to water the flock of their father.

**17** And the shepherds came and drove them away. And Moses rose up and saved them and watered their flock.

**18** And they came to Reuel their father, and he said, "Why have you come so quickly today?"

**19** And they said, "An Egyptian man delivered us from the hand of the shepherds, and he also drew water abundantly for us and watered the flock."

**20** And he said to his daughters, "And where is he? Why is it that you have left the man? Call him, that he may eat bread."

**21** And Moses was content to dwell with the man, and he gave Zipporah his daughter to Moses.

**22** And she bore a son, and he called his name Gershom, for he said, "I have been a sojourner in a foreign land."

**23** And it came to pass in those many days that the king of Egypt died. And the sons of Israel groaned because of the bondage and cried out, and their cry for help went up to God because of the bondage.

**24** And God heard their groaning, and God remembered His covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.

**25** And God saw the sons of Israel, and God knew.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~32r (PDF p. 62) continues from Exodus 1 into the opening of chapter 2, with the narrative of Moses' birth beginning in the right-hand column. The text flows onto folio ~32v (PDF p. 63), which carries the chapter through to its conclusion at verse 25 and into the opening of chapter 3. The Masorah parva is visible in the margins, with occasional interlinear annotations. The Vaticanus LXX (pp. 42-43) was consulted throughout; the Greek generally follows the Hebrew narrative closely, though with characteristic differences noted below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Ark" (v. 3):** Hebrew tevah is rendered "ark," the same word used for Noah's vessel in Genesis 6-9. This is a deliberate choice: the Hebrew text uses this rare word (occurring only in the Noah narrative and here) to create an unmistakable theological echo -- both are vessels of deliverance through water. The LXX uses thibis (a borrowing from Egyptian), which obscures this intertextual link. Most modern translations use "basket," which is interpretively accurate but loses the resonance. LOGOS preserves the formal equivalence and the echo.

2. **"That he was good" (v. 2):** Hebrew ki tov hu, rendered literally as "that he was good." This phrase deliberately echoes God's repeated verdict in Genesis 1 ("and God saw that it was good"), connecting the birth of Moses to the creation narrative. The LXX reads asteion auto ("that he was beautiful/fine"), interpreting the aesthetic dimension. LOGOS preserves the exact Hebrew phrasing to allow the reader to hear the Genesis echo.

3. **"Handmaid" vs. "maidens" (v. 5):** The Hebrew distinguishes between na'aroteyha ("her maidens," the attendants walking along the riverbank) and amatah ("her handmaid," the single servant sent to retrieve the ark). LOGOS preserves this distinction. Some translations render amatah as "her arm" (following an alternative reading), but the Leningrad Codex reading clearly supports "her handmaid," and this is confirmed by the LXX (ten abrau autes, "her attendant").

4. **"Children of the Hebrews" (v. 6):** Hebrew miyaldey ha'ivrim. The daughter of Pharaoh uses the term "Hebrews" (ivrim), the outsider designation for the Israelites, which is appropriate to her perspective. Rendered as "children of the Hebrews" to preserve the formal structure.

5. **"One in the wrong" (v. 13):** Hebrew larasha, rendered "the one in the wrong." The participle carries a judicial sense -- the one who is wicked or guilty in the dispute. This preserves the moral dimension of Moses' intervention without over-translating. The LXX uses to adikounti ("the one doing injustice").

6. **"A man, a prince and a judge" (v. 14):** Hebrew ish sar veshofet. The construction places "man" in apposition to "prince and judge," rendered faithfully. The response reveals that Moses' killing of the Egyptian is known and his authority is being challenged.

7. **"The well" (v. 15):** Hebrew ha-be'er, with the definite article -- "the well." This is the well of Midian, a significant location in the patriarchal narrative pattern (cf. Genesis 24:11, 29:2). The definite article suggests a known, central well, a gathering place. LOGOS preserves the article.

8. **"Drew water abundantly" (v. 19):** Hebrew daloh dalah, an emphatic infinitive absolute construction, rendered "drew water abundantly" to capture the intensification. The doubled verb form emphasizes the generosity or thoroughness of Moses' action.

9. **"God knew" (v. 25):** Hebrew vayyeda Elohim. The verse ends abruptly: "And God knew." No object is supplied in the Hebrew. This stark, objectless construction is theologically profound -- God's knowing is total and needs no specification. Some translations supply "their condition" or similar, but the MT leaves it unresolved, and LOGOS preserves this powerful open ending. The LXX adds egnosthe autois ("he was made known to them"), a significant theological reinterpretation that shifts the meaning from God's internal knowing to a revelatory act.

10. **"Groaned... cried out... cry for help" (v. 23):** Three distinct Hebrew terms are used: vayyei'anchu ("groaned"), vayyiz'aqu ("cried out"), and shav'atam ("their cry for help"). LOGOS preserves all three to maintain the building intensity of Israel's suffering, moving from inarticulate groaning to active outcry to a plea directed upward to God.

11. **Reuel (v. 18):** The father of the daughters is named Reuel here, though he is later called Jethro (Exodus 3:1, 18:1). LOGOS follows the MT in each instance without harmonization. The name Reuel means "friend of God."

### Consistent Term Renderings (Exodus Series)
| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Not "children of Israel" or "Israelites" |
| Par'oh | Pharaoh | Retained as title |
| hayeor | the River | Capitalized; the Nile specifically |
| tevah | ark | Same term as Noah's ark; not "basket" |
| amatah | handmaid | Distinguished from na'arot ("maidens") |
| Mosheh | Moses | "For I drew him (meshitihu) out of the water" |
| Gershom | Gershom | "Sojourner there" (ger sham) |
| Re'u'el | Reuel | "Friend of God"; later called Jethro |
| Tsipporah | Zipporah | "Bird" |
| befarekh | with harshness | Carried from ch. 1 |

— LOGOS
